title: What tm_devices Actually Is
body:
tm_devices is NOT a SCPI command list. It is a Python command framework that composes SCPI at runtime.
Key Point: SCPI strings do not exist as static data. They are built when a method is executed.
Think of tm_devices_full_tree.json as an AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) of the command language, not assembly code.
Commands are exposed as Python objects, not strings. Example: scope.commands.acquire.state.write("ON") internally sends 'ACQuire:STATE ON' over VISA.

title: Command Object Graph Structure
body:
Commands in tm_devices are structured as a Python object hierarchy:
- scope.commands — root command group
- scope.commands.acquire — acquisition subsystem
- scope.commands.acquire.state — state property
- scope.commands.acquire.state.write("ON") — sends SCPI
- scope.commands.ch[1].scale.write(1.0) — indexed channel access

Indexed nodes use bracket syntax: ch[1], math[2], source[1], meas[1].
Navigation: group → subgroup → ... → leaf → method (write/query).
code: scope.commands.acquire.state.write("ON")  # sends ACQuire:STATE ON

title: Method Types: write, query, set_and_verify, verify
body:
tm_devices methods:
- write(value) — Sends a SCPI SET command. e.g., scope.commands.ch[1].scale.write(1.0)
- query() — Sends a SCPI QUERY command, returns string. e.g., result = scope.commands.ch[1].scale.query()
- set_and_verify(value) — Writes then queries to confirm value was set correctly
- verify(expected) — Queries and checks that result matches expected value
- no_op — Placeholder, does nothing (exists in tree but not executable)

title: Indexed Nodes — ch[x], math[x], source[x]
body:
Many tm_devices command groups use indexed access:
- scope.commands.ch[1] — Channel 1
- scope.commands.ch[2] — Channel 2
- scope.commands.math[1] — Math waveform 1
- scope.commands.measurement.meas[1] — Measurement 1
- afg.commands.source[1] — AFG source channel 1

In the command browser, indexed nodes prompt the user to enter an index value.
In generated code, the index appears in brackets: ch[1], not ch1.
code: scope.commands.ch[1].scale.write(1.0)

title: Property vs Method Distinction
body:
In tm_devices:
- Properties are intermediate nodes in the command tree (e.g., scope.commands.acquire)
- Methods are executable endpoints (e.g., .write(), .query(), .set_and_verify())
- A property can have both sub-properties AND methods
- The full tree JSON marks nodes as GROUP (intermediate), LEAF (terminal), or METHOD (executable)
- Only METHOD nodes generate actual SCPI communication

title: What the Full Tree JSON Represents
body:
tm_devices_full_tree.json is a structural map of the tm_devices command object graph.
It represents: every command group, every valid subcommand, every indexed factory,
every callable method, and model-specific availability.
It does NOT represent literal SCPI strings.
Node types in the tree:
- GROUP: has children, navigate deeper
- LEAF: terminal node, has methods
- METHOD: executable (write, query, set_and_verify, verify, no_op)

title: Basic Connection by IP Address
body: Connect to a scope by IP address using DeviceManager:
code:
from tm_devices import DeviceManager
from tm_devices.drivers import MSO6B

with DeviceManager(verbose=True) as dm:
    dm.setup_cleanup_enabled = True
    dm.teardown_cleanup_enabled = True
    scope: MSO6B = dm.add_scope("192.168.0.1")
    # scope is now connected and ready

title: Connection by Hostname
body: Connect using instrument hostname instead of IP.
code: scope: MSO5 = dm.add_scope("MSO56-100083")

title: Connection with Alias
body: Add an alias for easier reference.
code: smu: SMU2450 = dm.add_smu("192.168.0.1", alias="my2450")

title: Multiple Device Types Simultaneously
body: Connect scope, AFG, PSU, SMU, AWG in same DeviceManager context.
code:
from tm_devices import DeviceManager
from tm_devices.drivers import MSO5, AFG3KC, PSU2200, SMU2470, AWG5K

with DeviceManager(verbose=True) as dm:
    scope: MSO5 = dm.add_scope("MSO56-100083")
    afg: AFG3KC = dm.add_afg("192.168.0.1")
    psu: PSU2200 = dm.add_psu("MODEL-SERIAL", connection_type="USB")
    smu: SMU2470 = dm.add_smu("192.168.0.2")
    awg: AWG5K = dm.add_awg("192.168.0.3")

title: Configuring PyVISA-py Backend
body: Use pure Python VISA backend instead of NI-VISA.
code:
from tm_devices import DeviceManager
from tm_devices.helpers import PYVISA_PY_BACKEND

with DeviceManager(verbose=True) as dm:
    dm.visa_library = PYVISA_PY_BACKEND
    scope: MSO6B = dm.add_scope("127.0.0.1")

title: Cleanup and Connection Lifecycle
body:
DeviceManager handles cleanup automatically in context manager:
- setup_cleanup_enabled = True → resets device on connect (*RST equivalent)
- teardown_cleanup_enabled = True → cleans up on disconnect
- Context manager (with statement) ensures close() is called
- Explicit: device_manager.close() if not using context manager
- Each device also has scope.close() for individual cleanup

title: USB, GPIB, and Serial Connections
body: Non-TCP/IP connection types.
code:
# USB
psu: PSU2200 = dm.add_psu("MODEL-SERIAL", connection_type="USB")

# GPIB
scope = dm.add_scope("1", connection_type="GPIB")  # GPIB address 1

# Serial
from tm_devices.helpers import SerialConfig
serial_config = SerialConfig(baud_rate=9600)
# dm.add_scope("COM3", connection_type="SERIAL", serial_config=serial_config)

title: Modern Oscilloscopes: MSO4B/5B/6B Series
body:
MSO6B: 4/6/8 channel, 1-10 GHz, flagship. Full tm_devices support.
MSO5B: 4/6/8 channel, 350 MHz-2 GHz. Full support.
MSO4B: 4 channel, 200 MHz-1.5 GHz. Full support.
Previous gen: MSO4, MSO5 (non-B). Also MSO2 (entry-level, 70-200 MHz).
All support: PyVISA, tm_devices, TekHSI (waveform only), VXI-11.

title: Legacy Oscilloscopes: DPO/MSO 5K/7K/70K
body:
DPO7000/C: 4ch, 500MHz-3.5GHz. DPO5000/B: 4ch, 350MHz-2GHz.
DPO70000 series (B/C/D/DX/SX): high-performance.
DSA70000 series. MSO70000 series.
Limited tm_devices support for older models. No TekHSI.
Best with PyVISA backend.

title: AFG Series (Function Generators)
body:
AFG3000 (AFG3K): 1/2ch, 250MHz, 2GS/s
AFG3000B (AFG3KB): Updated. AFG3000C (AFG3KC): Latest.
AFG31000 (AFG31K): 1/2ch, 250MHz, 14-bit resolution.
Full tm_devices support with source[1]/source[2] indexed access.

title: AWG Series (Arbitrary Waveform Generators)
body:
AWG5000: 2/4ch, 600MS/s-1.2GS/s. AWG5200: 2/4/8ch, 10GS/s.
AWG7000: 1/2ch, 10-20GS/s. AWG70000: 1/2ch, 25-50GS/s ultra high speed.
tm_devices classes: AWG5K, AWG5200, AWG7K, AWG70K.

title: Keithley SMU Series (Source Measure Units)
body:
PI-based (SCPI): SMU2400, SMU2450 (touchscreen), SMU2460 (7A high current), SMU2470 (1100V high voltage).
TSP-based: SMU2600, SMU2600B (dual-channel).
PI models use standard SCPI :SOURce:/:SENSe:/:OUTPut commands.
TSP models use Lua-based Test Script Processor.

title: Other Keithley Instruments
body:
PSU2200 series: Power supplies. Basic voltage/current control.
DMM6500, DMM7510: Digital multimeters. Voltage, current, resistance.
DAQ6510: Data acquisition. Multi-channel scanning.
All support PyVISA and tm_devices.

title: Basic Scope Setup Pattern
body: Configure horizontal, vertical, and trigger settings.
code:
# Horizontal
scope.commands.horizontal.scale.write(1e-6)  # 1 µs/div
scope.commands.horizontal.recordlength.write(10000)

# Vertical
scope.commands.ch[1].scale.write(0.5)  # 500 mV/div
scope.commands.ch[1].offset.write(0)
scope.commands.ch[1].coupling.write("DC")

# Trigger
scope.commands.trigger.a.edge.source.write("CH1")
scope.commands.trigger.a.edge.slope.write("RISE")
scope.commands.trigger.a.level.ch[1].write(0.5)

title: Acquisition Control Pattern
body: Start, stop, configure acquisition.
code:
scope.commands.acquire.state.write("OFF")  # Stop
scope.commands.acquire.mode.write("SAMple")
scope.commands.acquire.stopafter.write("SEQuence")
scope.commands.acquire.state.write("ON")  # Start single
scope.commands.opc.query()  # Wait for completion

title: Measurement Pattern
body: Add and read automated measurements.
code:
# Add frequency measurement on CH1
scope.commands.measurement.meas[1].type.write("FREQuency")
scope.commands.measurement.meas[1].source.write("CH1")

# Read measurement value
freq = scope.commands.measurement.meas[1].value.query()
print(f"Frequency: {freq}")

title: FastFrame Pattern
body: Enable and use FastFrame for multi-frame acquisition.
code:
# Enable FastFrame
scope.commands.horizontal.fastframe.state.write("ON")
scope.commands.horizontal.fastframe.count.write(100)

# Acquire
scope.commands.acquire.stopafter.write("SEQuence")
scope.commands.acquire.state.write("ON")
scope.commands.opc.query()

# Select and read specific frame
scope.commands.horizontal.fastframe.selected.write(1)
data = scope.commands.curve.query()

title: Search Pattern
body: Configure and query search results.
code:
# Configure edge search
scope.commands.search.search[1].trigger.a.edge.source.write("CH1")
scope.commands.search.search[1].trigger.a.edge.slope.write("RISE")

# Query total marks found
total = scope.commands.search.search[1].total.query()
print(f"Found {total} edges")

title: Screenshot Capture (tm_devices)
body: Use built-in save_screenshot method.
code:
# tm_devices has a built-in method
scope.save_screenshot("screenshot.png")
# This handles the full save+transfer+cleanup internally

title: Session Recall (tm_devices)
body: Recall instrument session.
code: scope.recall_session("C:/path/to/session.tss")

title: AFG Signal Generation Pattern
body: Configure and enable AFG output.
code:
afg.commands.source[1].frequency.fixed.write(1e6)  # 1 MHz
afg.commands.source[1].voltage.amplitude.write(1.0)  # 1 Vpp
afg.commands.source[1].function.shape.write("SINusoid")
afg.commands.output[1].state.write("ON")

title: SMU Voltage Source Pattern
body: Configure SMU as voltage source and measure current.
code:
# Configure as voltage source
smu.commands.source.function.write("VOLTage")
smu.commands.source.voltage.level.write(5.0)
smu.commands.source.voltage.range.auto.write("ON")
smu.commands.sense.current.protection.write(0.1)  # 100mA compliance

# Enable output
smu.commands.output.write("ON")

# Measure current
current = smu.commands.measure.current.query()
print(f"Current: {current} A")

# Disable output
smu.commands.output.write("OFF")

title: SMU IV Sweep Pattern
body: Sweep voltage and measure current for IV curve.
code:
import time
smu.commands.source.function.write("VOLTage")
smu.commands.output.write("ON")

voltages = [v * 0.1 for v in range(0, 51)]  # 0 to 5V
currents = []

for v in voltages:
    smu.commands.source.voltage.level.write(v)
    time.sleep(0.05)  # settle
    i = float(smu.commands.measure.current.query())
    currents.append(i)

smu.commands.output.write("OFF")

title: Waveform Data Transfer Pattern
body: Transfer waveform data from scope.
code:
# Configure data transfer
scope.commands.data.source.write("CH1")
scope.commands.data.encoding.write("RPBinary")
scope.commands.data.start.write(1)
scope.commands.data.stop.write(10000)

# Transfer
waveform = scope.commands.curve.query()

title: IEEE 488.2 Common Commands
body: Standard common commands available on all instruments.
code:
idn = scope.commands.idn.query()  # *IDN?
scope.commands.rst.write()  # *RST
scope.commands.cls.write()  # *CLS
scope.commands.opc.query()  # *OPC? — wait for completion

title: Error Handling Pattern
body: Check for errors after operations.
code:
# Check Event Status Register
esr = scope.commands.esr.query()
if int(esr) != 0:
    # Read error queue
    error = scope.commands.allev.query()
    print(f"Error: {error}")

title: TekAutomate tm_devices Code Generation
body:
When TekAutomate generates Python for tm_devices backend:
1. Imports: from tm_devices import DeviceManager; from tm_devices.drivers import MSO6B
2. Context manager: with DeviceManager(verbose=True) as device_manager:
3. Type hints: scope: MSO6B = device_manager.add_scope("ip")
4. Cleanup: automatic via context manager
5. If DRIVER_NAME not specified in XML, no type hint generated
code:
from tm_devices import DeviceManager
from tm_devices.drivers import MSO6B

with DeviceManager(verbose=True) as device_manager:
    device_manager.setup_cleanup_enabled = True
    device_manager.teardown_cleanup_enabled = True
    scope: MSO6B = device_manager.add_scope("192.168.0.1")
    
    # ... generated commands ...
    
    scope.close()
print("Done")

title: Backend Capability Validation
body:
When tm_devices backend is selected, TekAutomate enforces:
- scpi_write blocks are FORBIDDEN (use tm_devices blocks or python_code)
- scpi_query blocks are FORBIDDEN
- Must use: fastframe_enable, search_configure_edge, measurement_immediate, python_code, etc.
Generator detects violations and shows clear error with HOW TO FIX guidance.
Console output: 'BACKEND CAPABILITY VIOLATION DETECTED' with list of forbidden blocks per device.

title: tm_device_command Step Type
body:
The tm_device_command step in Steps UI:
- params.code: generated Python call (e.g., 'scope.commands.acquire.state.write("ON")')
- params.model: instrument model for context (e.g., 'MSO6B')
- params.description: human-readable description
Added to STEP_PALETTE with purple icon and color scheme.
Code is inserted directly into generated Python.

title: tm_devices Command Browser
body:
Full-screen hierarchical command browser:
- 70+ instrument models available
- Model search to quickly find instrument
- Tree navigation with visual node type indicators
- Indexed nodes prompt for index value
- Method nodes show arguments and generate code
- Breadcrumb path shows current location
- Back button for navigation
- Three overlays: main browser, index input, method arguments
Access: Add tm_devices Command step → Browse Commands, or Commands tab → purple tm_devices Browser button

title: Blockly Blocks for tm_devices
body:
Dedicated Blockly blocks for common tm_devices operations:
- fastframe_enable: STATE (ON/OFF)
- fastframe_set_count: COUNT (1-10000)
- fastframe_select_frame: CHANNEL, FRAME
- search_configure_edge: SEARCH_NUM, SOURCE, SLOPE
- search_query_total: SEARCH_NUM, VARIABLE
- measurement_immediate: TYPE, SOURCE, VARIABLE
- tm_devices_save_screenshot
- tm_devices_recall_session
These generate proper tm_devices Python code, not SCPI strings.
Preferred over python_code blocks for these operations.

title: The QString Problem and Fix
body:
QString is a SCPI data type for quoted string parameters.
When tm_devices sends: scope.commands.application.activate.write("TekExpress PCI")
It internally adds SCPI quotes: :APPLication:ACTivate "TekExpress PCI"
BUG: User input was getting double-quoted. Fix: tm_devices handles quoting automatically.
Rule: Provide plain Python strings to tm_devices methods. Never add SCPI quotes manually.

title: Shared Implementations and Mixins
body:
tm_devices.driver_mixins.shared_implementations provides:
- CommonPISystemErrorCheckMixin: PI-side error checking (expect_esr())
- CommonTSPErrorCheckMixin: TSP-side error checking
- IEEE4882Commands: Standard IEEE 488.2 commands (*IDN?, *RST, *OPC?, *CLS, etc.)
- LegacyTSPIEEE4882Commands, TSPIEEE4882Commands
TekAutomate doesn't instantiate these directly — consumed through official driver classes.

title: tm_devices Helpers Module
body:
tm_devices.helpers provides:
- Constants: PYVISA_PY_BACKEND, SYSTEM_DEFAULT_VISA_BACKEND
- Connection validation: check_network_connection, check_port_connection, check_visa_connection
- VISA utilities: create_visa_connection, detect_visa_resource_expression, get_visa_backend
- Value safety: validate_address, sanitize_enum, verify_values
TekAutomate uses backend constants for DeviceManager.visa_library selection.

title: PI (SCPI) vs TSP (Lua) Devices
body:
tm_devices supports two control interfaces:
- PI (Programmable Interface): Standard SCPI commands. Used by MSO scopes, AFGs, AWGs, SMU2400 series.
- TSP (Test Script Processor): Lua-based scripting. Used by SMU2600/2600B series, some Keithley instruments.
PI devices use colon-separated command paths: :SOURce:VOLTage:LEVel 5.0
TSP devices use Lua syntax: smu.source.levelv = 5.0
TekAutomate primarily generates PI/SCPI code. TSP support is limited.

title: SCPI ↔ tm_devices Conversion
body:
Bidirectional conversion between SCPI strings and tm_devices calls:

| SCPI Command | tm_devices Path | Method | Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| CH1:SCALE 1.0 | ch[1].scale | write | 1.0 |
| CH2:COUPLING DC | ch[2].coupling | write | DC |
| *IDN? | commands.idn | query | — |
| ACQuire:STATE ON | acquire.state | write | ON |
| MATH1:DEFINE "CH1+CH2" | math[1].define | write | "CH1+CH2" |
| HORIZONTAL:SCALE? | horizontal.scale | query | — |

Conversion utility: scpiToTmDevicesConverter.ts
Right-click context menu on SCPI blocks: "Convert to tm_devices Command"

title: When to Use tm_devices vs PyVISA
body:
Use tm_devices when:
- Working with modern MSO4B/5B/6B scopes and want Python API
- Need command validation and type checking
- Want IDE autocomplete for commands
- Building complex multi-step automation
- Need set_and_verify for reliable configuration

Use PyVISA when:
- Working with legacy instruments (DPO5K/7K/70K)
- Need maximum compatibility
- Working with measurements, search, histogram
- Simple send-command workflows
- Cross-platform compatibility critical
- Instrument not supported by tm_devices

PyVISA is the default/recommended choice for most users.
